[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Society

By Yiannis Gabriel. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul, 1983, 324 pp., $29.95.

Peter Loewenberg Ph.D.

Peter Loewenberg

Peter Loewenberg, Ph.D.
Los Angeles, California

Peter Loewenberg  

Freud's work is so rich in ideas and potential that it has appealed to thinkers with diverse political and ideological positions. Gabriel surveys what social theorists have done with the Freudian corpus, in each case selecting a perspective or part of it that they used for their own theory building and understanding. This book is a review of all the major statements Freud made about society and culture, followed by a survey of how various modern culture critics and social scientists have engaged with Freud and used him in their work.

Part One of the book is an examination of Freud's dualisms between body and mind, individual and society, normality and neurosis, culture and morality, science and philosophy, utopia and illusion, and psychoanalysis and culture. My reservation about this section is that it is largely static. Gabriel does not show Freud's ideas as they developed over time. The book often collapses the early Freud of the 1890's with his late cultural writings of the 1930's. If the book had incorporated the historical dimension, we could see Freud's thought in context as it evolved, progressed, and was reshaped in the phases of his development as a theorist, clinician, and culture critic.

Part Two plays off the utopian interpreters of Freud against the conservatives such as Philip Rieff and Talcott Parsons. Freud's writings, with their richness and ambiguity, offer ample justification for both readings. Freud builds in a tension between truth and pleasure—the gratification of passion inhibits the pursuit of the intellect, while the strengthening of reason tames the instincts.

I have reservations about this anthology on two accounts: its editorial commentary and some of the choices made for its 26 anthologized essays. Before elaborating my reservations, however, I should briefly describe the anthology, which is co-edited by Edith Kurzweil, a sociologist, and William Phillips, a literary critic and long-term editor of the Partisan Review. After collaborating on a short preface, the editors divided their tasks, Phillips writing the extended "General Introduction" and Kurzweil composing a preliminary commentary for each of the six comprehensive sections among which the essays are grouped: (1) Early Psychoanalytic Theory, (2) Literary Approaches and Theories, (3) Literary Applications, (4) Three Views of Kafka, (5) Two Views of Carroll, and (6) The French Connection.

The Psychiatric Treatment of Adolescents

Edited by Aaron H. Esman. New York: International Universities Press, 1983, xiii + 543 pp., $35.00.

John Schowalter M.D.

John Schowalter

John E. Schowalter, M.D.
New Haven, Connecticut

John Schowalter  

Adolescence is a period of life that seems to stir controversy naturally. Maybe this stems from memories of or identification with the uncertainties and rebelliousness that are so common for this developmental phase, but controversy and divisiveness there usually are. Some of the more obvious examples center on whether or not emotional turmoil is ubiquitous during adolescence, whether or not psychoanalysis is possible or useful during adolescence, and whether the training for the treatment for adolescents is best linked with the treatment of children, of adults, or should be considered a unique subspecialty. Because these debates have been going on so long and are usually counterproductive, it is with hopeful anticipation that one approaches a volume that promises to provide a unifying overview to the area of the psychiatric treatment of adolescents. This anticipation is especially high when the book is edited by an authority of the stature of Dr. Esman, a psychoanalyst who has had a long and successful career working with this age group.

Since this review is prepared for psychoanalysts, it will adhere to that particular perspective. I make this explicit at the beginning because I do not believe that the book was written primarily for a psychoanalytic audience, but for therapists who have some knowledge of psychoanalytic theory.

The 16 chapters are divided into sections devoted to the therapeutic approaches of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy, residential treatment, family therapy, group therapy, behavior therapy, and psychopharmacology, and a final section consists of three chapters summarizing respectively the individual, family, and behavior therapy approaches to anorexia nervosa. In the Introduction, Esman states he has "sought to present as fairly as possible a variety of points of view and theoretical perspectives" (p. xiii). As the book's title makes clear, however, this is a compilation of papers on the psychiatric, not psychoanalytic, treatment of adolescents.

[[RunningHead]]

Before the Pleasure Principle: Translation and its Vicissitudes

William Grossman M.D.

William Grossman

William Grossman  

August 17, 1985

In the course of Ornston's (1985) continuing interesting and valuable exploration of the systematic biases of Strachey's translation in the Standard Edition, Ornston offers an arresting translation of his own. Lustprinzip (p. 407) is rendered as "the principle of desire and delight."

While from the point of view of precise translation, "desire and delight" may, perhaps, be the most accurate English equivalent, some additional factors may be worth considering before it is assumed that the translation is Freud's conception. The foremost consideration is that Freud's (1880) translation of Mill's (1866) essay on Plato regularly renders "pleasure" as Lust. The idea of a principle of pleasure and pain regulating mind and behavior was not new and was prominent in the works of the Utilitarians. Freud was at least acquainted with the writings of Mill and Bain, among these. Thus, Lustprinzip was already an interpretation/translation of an established concept. Perhaps there was, as well, an established precedent for the translation of these concepts in the German philosophical literature.

Two other factors, at least, would also be of relevance. One would be the lack of precision in the use of "pleasure" by the English-speaking philosophers, such that, unless great specificity was required, "pleasure" meant any "positive" affect. Second, Freud's concept of the pleasure principle, as Schur (1966) has shown, has more than experiential referents. This consideration would call into question the value of overly precise translation in experiential terms.

These comments are certainly not conclusive, nor are they intended as such. Rather, they call attention to some additional issues in the translation of conceptual terms in Freud's writings. In particular, Freud's own understanding of English and the existence of conventions in translating standard concepts from one language to another pose intriguing questions.

ABSTRACT

This presentation reveals a little-known area of Robert Waelder's work. As his literary executor, I have been privileged with access to his unpublished material, notes, and correspondence. And, of course, I am familiar with his mode of thinking. I wish to pass on some of this knowledge.

What cannot be passed on, however, in some abstract intellectual way, are psychoanalytic principles themselves. Therefore, while this paper sheds light on a very particular aspect of Robert Waelder's work, it is also in some ways a cautionary tale, a plea that the reader understand what is behind the psychoanalytic point of view and its basic concepts—namely, clinical experience.

Pseudoquotes in Psychoanalytic Interventions

Donald Moss M.D.

Donald Moss

Donald Moss, M.D.
80 University Place
New York, New York 10003

Donald Moss  

ABSTRACT

The syntactical structure of any psychoanalytic intervention is itself overdetermined. As such, when the syntax of an analyst's intervention seems out of the ordinary, it may mark a moment of particular difficulty. One syntactical form used frequently by psychoanalysts is a pseudoquote. In this form, the analyst speaks for the patient, uses the pronoun "I," as though he were, for the moment, the voice of the patient.

Three such interventions are presented here from audio-recorded material. The interventions are examined in context, and some probable determinants are sketched. These determinants cluster around wishes and fears associated with "the difficult moment of self-other relatedness."

This somewhat empirical portion of the paper is followed by a theoretical discussion in which the development of thought, speech, and the ego are related to the question of whether pseudoquotes actually serve the psychoanalytic process.

Childhood Amnesia: A Conceptualization in Cognitive-Psychological Terms

Scott Wetzler Ph.D. and John Sweeney Ph.D.

John Sweeney

Scott E. Wetzler, Ph.D.
Psychology Department
Montefiore Hospital
111 East 210th Street
Bronx, New York 10467
John A. Sweeney, Ph.D.
Payne Whitney Clinic
New York Hospital
525 East 68th Street
New York, New York 10021

John Sweeney  

ABSTRACT

Childhood amnesia is defined operationally as the forgetting of early life events to a significantly greater degree than is accounted for by "normal" forgetting, which is an increasing recall decrement as time since occurrence increases. Three cognitive processes and their developmental changes are discussed which, when considered with psychodynamic factors, may broaden the understanding of childhood amnesia. It is argued that psychoanalytic theory and technique can benefit from the research and methods of cognitive psychology.

Judy Kantrowitz Ph.D.

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy L. Kantrowitz, Ph.D.
Brookline, Massachusetts

Judy Kantrowitz  

Research in personality assessment, especially when the theoretical assumptions about the organizational structures are based on a theory as complex as psychoanalysis, has been a formidable undertaking. The difficulties exist at two extremes. On the one hand, the in-depth case study method retains the richness and complexity of the clinical data in its descriptive report, but fails to take into account concerns for issues of reliability and validity. Theoretical constructs, such as those of psychoanalytic metapsychology, are difficult to translate into observable behavior, and agreement about the meaning of any particular piece of behavior is often subject to many differing interpretations, even when detailed clinical material is presented. In addition, the understanding of what exactly we mean when we use abstract psychoanalytic terms is itself often open to dispute. On the other hand, studies which have tried to rigorously apply the methods of natural science have tended to lose not only the richness of the clinical description, but often the meaning of the material. In their attempt to attain reliability, complex concepts are often translated into simplistic terms, which while they may be reliably observable, are far distant from the meaning of the original concept.

[[RunningHead]]

The Princess in the Tower: Zelda Fitzgerald's Creative Impasse

Elizabeth Waites Ph.D.

Elizabeth Waites

Elizabeth A. Waites, Ph.D.
206 South Main Street (Suite 200)
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48104

Elizabeth Waites  

ABSTRACT

Gifted women often find it difficult to integrate an acceptable social image with an autonomous artistic identity. This paper explores the ambitions and conflicts of Zelda Fitzgerald, who aspired to be an artist in several fields, but whose fame is largely contingent on the role she played in the life of her husband, F. Scott Fitzgerald. Zelda's flamboyant public pose, which served as a model for many of her husband's literary characters, is interpreted as an attempt to organize a consistent identity configuration by means of constant appeals for applause from an admiring audience. But although this configuration provided some stability and cohesion for her during her twenties, it was a superficial and vulnerable structure which failed to support her serious artistic aspirations. An examination of the separation conflicts and difficulties in internalization which contributed to Zelda's dilemma leads to a consideration of the role of the father in the organization of differentiated female achievements. Although Zelda Fitzgerald's pathology was extreme, her struggles suggest useful insights into the intrapsychic and social barriers commonly confronting creative women.

The Jokes of Sigmund Freud. A Study in Humor and Jewish Identity

By Elliott Oring. Philadelphia: Univ. Pennsylvania Press, 1984, 151 pp., $14.95.

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
Harrison, New York

Mark Kanzer  

Elliott Oring surveys Freud's Jewish jokes from the standpoint of their analytic significance, especially in relation to his Jewish identity. Freud informed Fliess on June 12, 18971 that he had begun to make a "significant" collection of Jewish jokes, a trend that coincided with his collection of dreams and the beginnings of self-analysis. The relation between the mental processes in dreams and jokes also drew his interest, and he referred to the wittiness of many dreams—e.g., the element of absurdity, based on embittered criticism, which permeated both.

[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference in Theory and Practice

Robert Tyson M.D. and Owen Renik M.D.

Owen Renik

Robert Tyson, M.D.
Owen Renik, M.D.
2618-A Jackson Street
San Francisco, California 94115

Owen Renik  

ROBERT TYSON INTRODUCED THE PANEL by reviewing the evolution of psychoanalytic thinking about countertransference. Freud's explicit remarks on the subject were few, though he seemed to refer to countertransference implicitly in various places when speaking about interferences with the analyst's attunement to his own unconscious responses. Freud regarded countertransference as an impediment to analytic work. He pointed first to manifestations of unconscious countertransference, and later added that countertransference enactment could involve conscious thought, citing the example of giving in to tender feelings toward a patient. Initially, Freud recommended self-analysis as the primary method of dealing with countertransference. Subsequently, he came to believe that self-analysis alone is insufficient, and suggested that training analysis be considered a requirement.

Since Freud, psychoanalytic ideas about countertransference have developed in four basic directions. The first has been expansion from a narrow focus on the analyst's unconscious responses to a view that encompasses preconscious and conscious responses to the analytic situation as well. A second direction, closely related to the first, has been the shift from Freud's limited definition of countertransference as the analyst's reactions to the patient's transferences to him, to a broader usage that includes virtually all the analyst's feelings about and attitudes toward the patient, together with their many and varied determinants. Some analysts have even considered the concept of countertransference too confining and ambiguous, and have suggested putting it aside. Much of the trend toward expansion has arisen from psychoanalytic work with more disturbed patients, in which the analyst's personal reactions often play a prominent role from the

[[RunningHead]]

Ernest Jones. Freud's Alter Ego.

By Vincent Brome. London: Caliban, 1982, 250 pp., £12.

Ernest Wolf M.D.

Ernest Wolf

Ernest S. Wolf, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Ernest Wolf  

Ernest Jones was a passionate man who lived and loved life to the fullest. When questioning himself, like a good psychoanalyst should, about his motives for writing Free Associations, his autobiographical account of the "Memories of a Psychoanalyst," a rather unexpected answer came to him: "Gratitude for life." Experiencing a deep need to say so, to tell someone so, and to thank someone, Jones tells us he indulged the illusion of prolonging that life by printed pages that might survive his bodily dissolution. Yet Jones recognized that even such a candid confession of his deepest motives for gratification—one might say of his narcissistic fantasies of immortal joy—did not justify his autobiographical effort. That justification he found in having assisted at and recorded certain scientific events to which, he was persuaded, history would ascribe "a very considerable importance" in the development of human thought. The autobiographical record itself thus became an expression of this gratitude (Jones, 1959, p. 9).

Indeed, while Jones' lust for life, especially his sexual and love life, may seem to have drawn excessive attention from his contemporaries as well as from his current biographer, his life found meaning and fulfillment only in his passionate dedication to psychoanalysis: "To me it is clear," he wrote to Freud, "that I owe my career, my livelihood, my position, and my capacity of happiness in marriage—in short everything—to you and the work you have done" (Clark, 1980, p. 260). This dedication to Freud and to psychoanalysis found its triumphant expression in the truly crowning achievement of his life—almost universally recognized as one of the outstanding biographies of our age—his monumental The Life and Work of Sigmund Freud.

737

[[RunningHead]]

Hoelderlin. Weder Die These Vom Edlen Simulanten

By Uwe Henrik Peters. Hamburg: Rowohlt Taschenbuch Verlag, 1982, 239 pp.

William Niederland M.D.

William Niederland

William G. Niederland, M.D.
Englewood, New Jersey

William Niederland  

Peters, professor of neuropsychiatry at the University of Cologne, offers a study of the life, work, and illness of Johann Christian Friedrich Hoelderlin (1770-1843), a German poet born in the obscure southwest German village of Lauffen on the Neckar river. Though a descendent of a long line of Protestant clergy, his name, poetry, and creative reputation sank into almost complete obscurity in the nineteenth century. Hoelderlin was rediscovered in our time and acclaimed as a great elegiac poet. Today we know that his writings influenced the sweeping rhythms of Stefan George, Rainer Maria Rilke, and Georg Trakl, in part even the selection of certain Lieder by Johannes Brahms.

With the expectation of becoming a clergyman himself, Hoelderlin studied theology, philosophy, and Greek civilization at the University of Tuebingen (1788-1793), where he came to know Hegel and Schelling; shortly thereafter, in Jena, he met Schiller, Goethe, and Fichte. He formed a Dichterbund, that is, a brotherhood of poets which, once a week, over a glass of wine or beer, recited contemporary literary works, sang the Marseillaise, and extolled ancient Greece as the fountain of artistic creation, immortal philosophy, and political liberty. With Schiller's aid, Hoelderlin, under the title of Magister, became a tutor of young children and completed his novel Hyperion, one of his best known creations among his numerous and often moving works of art. He wrote elegies, hymns, odes, dramatic and philosophical essays of great linguistic beauty, coined impressive neologisms and, always living in poverty, prepared in 1799 to publish a literary journal to earn enough money in order to continue composing poetry without having to tutor. This project, however, came to naught.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Infant Research

By Joseph, D. Lichtenberg. Hillsdale, N.J.: Analytic Press, 1983, 262 pp., $29.95.

Philip Spielman M.D.

Philip Spielman

Philip Spielman, M.D.
Berkeley, California

Philip Spielman  

It is surprising that there is so little consideration in our literature of the exciting work in infant and neonatal research. This is particularly puzzling in view of our concern with the widening scope of analysis, with formative preoedipal and preverbal experience, and with reconstruction in analytic work. In this book, Dr. Lichtenberg sets out to remedy this neglect, and does so with enthusiasm and thoroughness. This is not an edited collection of papers on infant research; rather, it is a unified presentation reflecting the author's devotion to integrating these research findings. A special interest in the development of the sense of the self guides this work.

The book is organized in four sections. Three of them—"The Neonate, The First Year of Life, and The Second Year of Life—constitute a little over half the book. In these pages Lichtenberg addresses "the challenge for psychoanalytic theory from neonate research," the development of the sense of the self and of the object, self-object differentiation in the first year, and the development of fantasy, emotion, memory, psychic representation, and defense. The final section of the book, Applications, is concerned with the applications of the findings to problems of theory and technique.

215

Portraits of the Artist

By John E. Gedo. New York: Guilford Press, 1983, 304 pp., $20.00.

Aaron Esman M.D.

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman, M.D.
New York City

Aaron Esman  

227

Although some of Freud's early followers made halting attempts at the application of his concepts to biography, it remained for the master himself to undertake the first major effort in that direction in his Leonardo essay of 1910. Strikingly, despite the primacy of his interest in literature, the prototypic Renaissance genius to whose life and work Freud first turned his mind (and with whom he clearly identified) was, above all, a painter—arguably the greatest of his or any age. It was, indeed, the Leonardo monograph that began that shaky and ambivalent love affair between psychoanalysis and the history of art that persists to our day and that continues to engage the interest of intrepid and incautious scholars and dilettantes in both fields.

The products of the convergence of these orbits have tended to fall into four general categories—largely following the evolution of psychoanalytic theory itself. First, as exemplified by Leonardo, came the efforts to demonstrate the validity of psychosexual theories by finding in the imagery of works of art symbolic representations of the classic Freudian complexes (this applied, of course, to literary works as well). Second were those studies which, like Greenacre's remarkable reconstructive tours de force, sought to answer the riddle of the developmental origins of creativity. Third were the ego-psychological and integrative essays such as those of Kris and Ehrenzweig that sought to cast light on the psychology of the creative process itself. And more recently we have seen works such as those of Peter Gay which attempt to set all these approaches within a broad historical and social context.

John Gedo, whose previous contributions to the literature have been distinguished as much by the high level of their ambition as by their erudition, scholarship, and conceptual daring, seeks in this collection of essays to attempt an integration of such a synthetic view with his own revisionist approach to psychoanalytic theory. His route toward that goal takes him through a critical review of earlier psychoanalytic studies of creativity, followed by accounts of some clinical experiences with a number of patients whom he considers to have been "creative" in a variety of fields (not all, despite the promise of his title, artistic). He then proceeds with what appears to be the heart of the book, a group of psychobiographic studies of major creative figures—Van Gogh, Gauguin, Picasso, Caravaggio, Nietzsche. Finally, in a considerable stretch of his theme, he devotes four chapters to a detailed review of the Freud-Jung relationship and to Jung's status as (in Gedo's view) the creator of a religious system.

[[RunningHead]]

Response to an Article by Leo Rangell

Robert Langs M.D.

Robert Langs

Robert Langs  

September 30, 1985

Leo Rangell (1985), in "On the Theory of Theory in Psychoanalysis and the Relation of Theory to Psychoanalytic Therapy" (This Journal, 33:59-92), alludes to my work at three points in his paper, and in each instance he misrepresents my position. Oddly enough, my own position on many of the issues raised by Rangell is in many instances close to his own thinking.

Rangell (pp. 64 and 65) states that I support Bion's proposal that the analyst enter the analytic hour without memory, desire, or understanding, only to immediately contradict myself by proposing that the analyst should also have silent hypotheses and specific recollections as guiding principles to generate clinical predictions. Rangell proposes that this is a gross contradiction that remains unquestioned by my "attentive and impressed audience."

The citation alluded to by Rangell clearly shows that the statement I make is that the analyst should enter each session intially without desire, memory, or understanding, but that he is "quickly directed toward silent hypotheses, free-floating associations, a bit of theoretical thinking, specific recollections about a particular patient, and such, responding as much as possible to the patient's associations" (italics added). Thus, I am attempting to indicate that theory prejudices listening, and yet remains indispensable to the task of meaningfully organizing the material.

494

The Evolution of Group Analysis

Edited by Malcolm Pines. London: Routledge, Kegan Paul, 1983, 393pp., $35.00.

Abraham Zaleznik

Abraham Zaleznik

Abraham Zaleznik, D.C.S.
Lexington, Massachusetts

Abraham Zaleznik  

Necessity is the mother of invention, an observation thoroughly sustained in explaining the origins of group therapy. World War II provided the necessity, and military psychiatrists, many of whom were analytically trained, the impetus to begin a new form of treatment for emotional disturbances. Faced with the enormous task of dealing with large numbers of patients, and the impossibility of applying individual psychotherapy in the face of these numbers, the analytically oriented psychiatrists brought together patients in small groups and, thus, gave birth to group psychotherapy.

For psychoanalysts in the military, such as Michael Foulkes, it became important to think of group therapy as an application of psychoanalysis albeit "beyond the couch." This work, in turn, coincided with the extensions of the therapeutic idea and, perhaps, ideology to the family, industry, and the community.

240

Renato Almansi

Renato J. Almansi, M.D.
New York, New York

Renato Almansi  

The conceptual origins of Freud's anthropological opus, its development, its relation to the discoveries of clinical psychoanalysis, the reactions it created in the world of anthropology, its merits and its shortcomings, and the present evaluation of his work in this field are detailed with the greatest care in this extremely well-informed and scholarly book.

Wallace feels that Freud's anthropological concepts are not peripheral, but an integral part of his total corpus, and that they "even influenced some of his clinical-psychological tenets, in particular his concept of projection, omnipotence of thoughts, and neurosis as atavism or survival." On the other hand, his psychological discoveries and the development of clinical psychoanalysis exerted a very strong influence on anthropology by calling attention to certain previously ignored phenomena such as childhood behavior, dreams and projection, by permitting a better understanding of the main operating tools of anthropologists, and by bringing about a mutually fruitful collaboration between clinician and anthropologist.

725

The Place and Value of Bestophilia in Perversions

Daniel Traub-Werner M.D.

Daniel Traub-Werner

Daniel Traub-Werner, M.D.
586 Eglinton Avenue East
Suite 602
Toronto, Ontario
Canada M4P 1P2

Daniel Traub-Werner  

ABSTRACT

An attempt is made to place bestophilia in cultural, clinical, and theoretical perspective. Archeological and anthropological documentation and records of the widespread appearance in mythology of this practice are cited. This archaic form of perversion is still practiced in rural environments and isolated communities. The treatment of a man who grew up in such an environment gave the author the opportunity to study this form of perversion. The correlation of clinical findings with culturally sanctioned bestophilia provides a bridge between the understanding of psychopathology and anthropological variation.

By Otto F. Kernberg. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1984, 381 pp., $35.00.

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
San Francisco, California

Robert Wallerstein  

The appearance now of the fourth in the sequence of Otto Kernberg's closely argued and incrementally reasoned books on the phenomena of the borderline personality organization and of pathological narcissism—their diagnosis, their prognosis, and their effective therapeutic management—warrants an appraisal of Kernberg's overall impact on the psychoanalytic world as theoretician and clinician in addition to the specific opportunity it provides to assess the place of this newest volume in the intellectual unfolding of his conceptions of the nature of our psychoanalytic understanding of these sicker patients. This is so because it seems quite clear by now that there is probably no psychoanalytic author more widely read or quoted in the world today both within specifically psychoanalytic circles and in the far wider arena of the psychoanalytically influenced world of mental health professionals;1 nor is any psychoanalytic author more squarely placed in relation to our ubiquitous clinical concerns with these new paradigmatic patients of our time—the borderline and the narcissistic—who so crowd our consulting rooms while simultaneously so bedeviling our therapeutic efforts.

Yet despite Kernberg's secure eminence in these regards, such an appraisal is neither simple nor uncontroversial.

1For example, in a recent informal survey of the psychoanalytic literature as represented in three of our leading psychoanalytic journals (International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, and Psychoanalytic Quarterly) from 1969 to 1982, that was carried out by the Committee on Scientific Activities of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 14 authors accounted for 36 of the 60 most frequently cited articles. Of these 14, Kernberg authored six of the articles, Mahler and Bowlby four each, and the other 11 most popular authors, two each.711

Working with Disadvantaged Parents and their Children

By Sally Provence and Audrey Naylor. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1983, 218 pp., $21.50.

Henri Parens M.D.

Henri Parens

Henri Parens, M.D.
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

Henri Parens  

Foremost, the work reported by Provence and Naylor enlarges the field of infant psychiatry, proposes an interventional strategy, and makes recommendations for the implementation of services to disadvantaged infants and their families, both of which we know to be greatly needed and to be sound. In addition, it is important for psychoanalysis.

743

It is noteworthy that a number of leaders in the field of infant psychiatry, which includes the senior author, are child psychoanalysts. We have to ascribe this to two factors at least: (1) the clinical finding by practitioners and clinical infant observers that many infants show evidence of emotional strain, symptoms, and disordered development; (2) discontinuity theory notwithstanding, Freud's genetic point of view, Erikson's epigenetic theory, and the more recently evolved developmental point of view, strongly corroborated by clinical work, all state the relevance of emotional development during the first three years of life—infancy—for all subsequent psychological development. In his last work, An Outline of Psychoanalysis, Freud noted that "the earliest cathexes are indelible." Both factors, complemented by much recent exciting psychoanalysis-relevant infancy research, have compelled clinicians to focus on and establish a sector of our field, infant psychiatry. For two decades now, Provence has been at the forefront of this field.

In this book Provence and Naylor detail a service strategy for infants at risk of maldevelopment and parents at risk for parenting difficulties. They report their five-year effort in working with seventeen disadvantaged families, each with a subject infant. They detail the types of service they rendered these families, namely, pediatric care, developmental evaluations, social services, and providing day care and preschool facilities. Although some examples are given of all services rendered, specific interventional strategies are given only sketchily, one of the major regrets I experienced about the book.

LEAVY'S PAPER, "MALE HOMOSEXUALITY RECONSIDERED," began with the observation that most psychoanalytic studies regard homosexuality as a developmental deviation, originating in a pathological context that can be reconstructed in the transference. Leavy reviewed Freud's ideas about the negative Oedipus complex and noted that subsequently: "The centrality of the oedipal development yielded to the finding of more and more preoedipal elements, the oedipal serving, as so often in psychopathological development, as a decisive turning point, in which precursor states become definitively summated." Many analysts assume that preoedipal influences also lead to global psychopathology, such as narcissistic or prepsychotic character structure. These analysts believe that homosexual men suffer from such pathology.

Leavy noted that the accepted models of homosexuality assume that heterosexuality "being normative for reproductive life, is generally normative too." Homosexuality is conceptualized as the effect of a failed heterosexuality. He suggested that the commonly held view that heterosexuality is normal would be understandably congenial to heterosexual analysts "for whom personally the kind of sexual experience homosexuals claim to enjoy would be grotesque as well as unsatisfying. The analyst along with the layman might see in the reported forms of homosexual union only frustrated mimicking of the intercourse 'normally' practiced by men with women. Consequently, since both partners are of the same sex, one at least must be engaging in an alien activity and holding to a spurious representation of himself. This judgment, springing uncritically from a heterosexual position, does not suddenly become neutral when the analyst reports confirmatory data from the patient's associations; selective emphasis may skew the data toward the side of the symptomatic, and away from

ABSTRACT

Historical, clinical, and conceptual remarks are presented regarding countertransference and the theory of psychoanalytic technique, preliminary to and in the context of discussing the contributions to the panel, "Countertransference in Theory and Practice" (Tyson, 1984; Loewald, 1984; Jacobs, 1984; Dewald, unpublished). In addition, special emphasis is given to problems of countertransference in training and supervision which may be prototypes for later difficulties in analytic work.

Countertransference Evolution in Theory and Practice

Robert Tyson M.D.

Robert Tyson

Robert L. Tyson, M.D.
Suite 102
3252 Holiday Court
La Jolla, California 92037

Robert Tyson  

ABSTRACT

A framework is suggested for conceptualizing countertransference, based on expansion of the concept emerging subsequent to Freud's original view of the phenomenon: (1) from Ucs to Cs, (2) from reactions to transference to all reactions, (3) from the analyst's neurosis to the analyst's functioning, (4) from self-analysis to self-scrutiny, (5) from obstacle to contribution. Particular attention is called to the advantages of maintaining the distinction between the patient's transference and the analyst's countertransference; the importance for successful psychoanalytic work of being aware of the subtleties of countertransference in work with neurotic patients, especially in contrast to the blatant countertransference experiences more disturbed patients thrust upon the analyst; the need for further investigation of the relations between the analyst's empathy, regression, and countertransference; the lack of understanding of and information about the homosexual countertransference, based on insufficient knowledge of the mechanisms of resistance to self-analysis, among other reasons; and the need for more reliable information about the limits of and indications for using countertransference responses in particular kinds of clinical situations, whether for informing the patient as to the analyst's responses to him, for informing the analyst in the interpretive process, or in formulating reconstructions. A clinical example provides an illustration of the complexity of countertransference-transference interaction and of the impact of countertransference on the transference.

[[RunningHead]]

When Interpretation Masquerades as Explanation

Donald Spence Ph.D.

Donald Spence

Donald P. Spence, Ph.D.
Department of Psychiatry Rutgers Medical School UMDNJ
Piscataway, New Jersey 08854

Donald Spence  

ABSTRACT

The principal goal of the clinical interpretation (and of the larger clinical narrative) is to bring about insight and change in the patient, and not to present a reasoned argument that relies on public data and shared rules of evidence and logic. When the clinical account is transposed to the public domain and presented as a form of explanation, it is no longer designed for the benefit of one individual but must now be accessible to all. We are still under the shadow of Freud's five famous cases which are literary landmarks of exposition and persuasion. As a result, we are less sensitive to what happens when interpretations are substituted for explanations. The time has come to develop a new genre and a new mode of clinical reporting that would allow the reader to participate in the argument, allow him to evaluate the proposed links between evidence and conclusion (instead of relying on the authority of the analyst-author), and open up the clinical report to the possibilities of refutation, disconfirmation, and falsification.

1938 Scott Street

Shahla Chehrazi  

ABSTRACT

This paper examines the differences between early psychoanalytic views of female development and current views, illustrating that recent suggested revisions offer an updated preliminary theoretical framework. The clinical implications of the differing views are discussed, and examples (two from the psychotherapy of children and a third from the psychoanalysis of a young woman) are offered to demonstrate particular clinical issues, such as penis envy and genital anxiety. It is postulated that the outcome of psychoanalytic treatment of women is markedly affected by the different clinical formulations and conceptualizations that result from different views. Further, the psychoanalytic terminology can contribute in subtle ways to ambiguity, distortion, and lack of clarity regarding female psychology.

On Countertransference Enactments

Theodore Jacobs M.D.

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore J. Jacobs, M.D.
17 East 96th Street
New York, New York 10128

Theodore Jacobs  

ABSTRACT

This communication focuses on the relation of countertransference to psychoanalytic technique, calling attention not to the more obvious forms of countertransference that have been commented on by previous writers on the subject, but to its subtler ones. Often well camouflaged within the framework of traditional, time-tested techniques, this aspect of countertransference may attach itself to our way of listening and thinking about patients, to our efforts at interpretation, to the process of working through, or to the complex issue of termination. Less recognizable than its more boisterous counterpart and in some respects less tangible, this side of the problem of countertransference is no less important. For it is precisely those subtle, often scarcely visible countertransference reactions, so easily rationalized as parts of our standard operating procedures and so easily overlooked, that may in the end have the greatest impact on our analytic work.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Time

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob A. Arlow, M.D.
120 East 36th Street (Apt. 1-H)
New York, New York 10016

Jacob Arlow  

ABSTRACT

Psychoanalysis is fundamentally related to time because it is an effort to understand how disturbances in the present are determined by events in the past. Technically, we know that the patient who is reporting immediate perceptions is not aware of the passage of time, but he becomes self-conscious as undesirable elements threaten to appear in his associations. Time is not sensed by direct awareness, nor is it an agent of action or events. Various functions of the ego influence how time is experienced consciously, leading to phenomena such as déjà vu, a sensation of timelessness, misjudgment of time duration, the experience of premonition. Psychoanalysis more than any other discipline sheds light on the coexistence of past, present, and future, as influenced by unconscious fantasy thinking. The analyst's understanding of the patient's associations is guided by temporal factors such as context and contiguity, succession of similar or opposite elements. Basically, the self is a time-bound concept; identity implies that a self is the same entity at different points in time. There is a deep-seated rebellion against the tyranny of time, beginning with need frustration in the infant and culminating in the knowledge that man is destined to lose the struggle against death.

Can Psychoanalysis Find its Self

W. Meissner S.J.

W. Meissner

W. W. Meissner, S.J.
129 Mount Auburn Street
Cambridge, Massachusetts 02138

W. Meissner  

ABSTRACT

There is a place for the theory of the self in psychoanalysis. To justify that claim, I return to an era in the development of psychoanalytic thinking about the self that preceded Kohut's formulations and that has set itself on a course of evolving a concept of the self more congruent with extant structural theory. I try to articulate the potential role of such a self-concept in connection with the issues of personal agency and subjectivity, complex personal qualities, areas of the complex interaction and integration of intrapsychic structures, object relations, narcissism, and internalization. The intent of this discussion is to facilitate the further development and articulation of the psychoanalytic theory of the self, particularly in terms of its integration with structural theory, to promote clearer thinking and more effective clinical efforts.

A Developmental View of the Psychoanalytic Process. Follow-Up Studies and their Consequences

by Nathan Schlessinger and Fred P. Robbins, New York: International Universities Press, 1983, 228 pp. $25.00.

Stanley Weiss M.D.

Stanley Weiss

Stanley S. Weiss, M.D.
Denver, Colorado

Stanley Weiss  

The Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis has inaugurated a new monograph series, Emotions and Behavior. In this first monograph, Schlessinger and Robbins present their research, carried out over the past decade, on the analytic process and the evaluation of analytic change and outcome—a most timely and significant topic. The study of the psychoanalytic process has been somewhat neglected by psychoanalytic educators, yet the concept involves basic issues of theory and technique, and it often mobilizes questioning and controversy among analysts.

Advanced candidates were offered a course on the assessment of completed analyses from the Institute Clinic. The course evolved into an ongoing group research project; their clinical studies and theoretical implications are the subject matter of the monograph.

The initial step in the research was to devise a method of assessment of the analytic process based on clinic investigation and follow-up research. The group decided to study the first few hours of the analysis, the point in analysis when termination was decided on, the last few hours in the analysis, and four to six follow-up sessions two to five years after termination. Measurements of intrapsychic change was based on a study of ego functions as viewed at these critical points in the analysis. The data were organized around the nature of the alliance, the Oedipus complex, the defense transference, and dreams.

responseSet.title: Coming of Age in Shakespeare
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Coming of Age in Shakespeare

Bennett Simon M.D.

Bennett Simon

Bennett Simon, M.D.
West Newton, Massachusetts

Bennett Simon  

By Marjorie Garber. London & New York: Methuen, 1981, 248 pp., $30.00.

MAN'S ESTATE: MASCULINE IDENTITY IN SHAKESPEARE. By Coppelia Kahn. Berkeley & Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1981, 238 pp., $21.00.

What makes Shakespeare so appealing to analysts is precisely what has made him have such a perennial, universal appeal—his ability to portray a variety of human conflictual situations, in all their complexity, and his rich, multilayered, multifaceted language. It is language that conveys so much of the ambiguity and unconscious intentionality in human communication.

The two works reviewed here emphasize a particular aspect of Shakespeare's works, namely the processes and ideals of maturity that are embodied in his works. Each utilizes certain psychoanalytic perspectives in delineating and showing the significance of these patterns of maturation in Shakespeare, and generally speaking, use these perspectives in a sensible and productive way. The results are readings of Shakespeare that are definitely enriching to psychoanalysts and, I believe, to specialists in Shakespeare as well to the general audience of Shakespeare lovers. They enlarge our conceptions of maturity and they embody the spirit of Shakespeare whereby it is not only his characters who may mature but his audience, too.

The works differ in their emphases in a number of ways. Like the frequent twinned pairs among Shakespeare's characters, each is impressive in its own right, and together, they marvelously complement each other. The Garber book draws upon a much more general and conceptually looser framework, and the Kahn book utilizes a more formal and formidable set of psychoanalytic hypotheses. For both, however, the core assumption about maturation is that to mature is to experience conflict and its resolution.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comment on "When Interpretation Masquerades as Explanation"

Jessica Benjamin Ph.D.

Jessica Benjamin

Jessica Benjamin  

August 5, 1985

Spence's paper, "When Interpretation Masquerades as Explanation" (This Journal, 34[1]) makes even clearer what the conclusion of his book on Narrative Truth and Historical Truth implies: that he is not content with interpretive understanding. Indeed, he retains a hope for a science of the mind and aims to create conditions under which we could still salvage explanation. Spence now turns to what he sees as the main obstacle to explanation: the inadequacy of our clinical evidence as a basis for explanation. The alternatives he presents—either we establish replicability in our data or admit that our knowledge is idiosyncratic and random—implicitly reaffirm the scientific criteria of truth. Thus, a contradiction arises between the platform from which Spence makes his criticisms and the one from which he offers solutions.

In his current paper, Spence reviews the idea of narrative coherence as a form of esthetic truth for psychoanalysis from the standpoint of scientific criteria. He points out that in the presentation of case material, narrative fit may be used to suggest that the analyst has provided an explanation of the data where he has merely organized it. A rhetorically powerful narration—as exemplified by the Dora case—can create the impression that one has found the cause of symptoms, identified the links between past events or fantasies and current ones. His suggestion for improvement focuses on the idea of replicability and conviction created by expanded evidence.

Comment on Spence's paper delivered at the New York University Postdoctoral Psychology Colloquium, October, 1984.

483

[[RunningHead]]

The Revival of "Dora": Advances in Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique

Jerry Jennings Ph.D.

Jerry Jennings

Jerry L. Jennings, Ph.D.
1033 Collings Avenue (Apt. 6)
Collingswood, New Jersey 08107

Jerry Jennings  

ABSTRACT

For nearly six decades after its publication in 1905, Freud's remarkable case of Dora remained untouched by critical comment. However, beginning in the early 1970's, an abundance of articles began to appear, which focused exclusively on the Dora case. The present paper reviews the literature of this so-called "Dora revival" in order to explain the historical and theoretical reasons leading to this extraordinary burst of research. Above all, two vital developments in the psychoanalytic discipline created the climate that fostered the Dora revival. First, there was a revolutionary change in attitude toward the phenomenon of countertransference: in contrast to the classical view of countertransference as a disruptive interference in treatment, analysts increasingly regarded countertransference as a pervasive and natural process, which could be potentially utilized to enhance understanding of the patient's unconscious conflicts and defenses. Second, there was enormous and rapid growth of a comprehensive psychoanalytic theory of adolescence and its treatment. Thus, based on a more favorable attitude toward countertransference, and a much improved understanding of the unique problems of adolescence, psychoanalysts could reexamine and better understand the decisive events that contributed to Freud's abortive analytic treatment of Dora.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reconstruction and Perceptual Experience: Further Thoughts on Psychoanalytic Listening

Evelyne Schwaber M.D.

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber, M.D.
4 Welland Road
Brookline, Massachusetts 02146

Evelyne Schwaber  

ABSTRACT

Freud's monumental shift to the fantasy theory heralded the view that "it is psychical and not material reality" which is our sole domain of inquiry. Yet despite theoretical agreement and cogent technical admonitions against concerning ourselves with absolute or "external" truths, psychoanalytic listening betrays a stance in which the analyst attunes to a reality other than that of the patient's inner world, assuming the position of arbiter—even if a silent one—of what is or is not "distorted" in the patient's perceptual experience. The central impact of perception as a significant component of the patient's inner experience—past and present—goes unattended. Clinical examples from differing theoretical persuasions are reviewed to demonstrate this occurrence.

An alternative mode of listening is considered which assumes an underlying shift in outlook, suspending any notion that we can "know" what is "correct" in the patient's perception, while sharpening attunement to its clinical impact—as may be evidenced by a shift in affect or state, a turn of phrase, or the transient appearance of a symptom or old behavior. It is argued that such a stance will lead to a more singular focus on the patient's psychic reality, permitting the emergence of a deepening realm of psychic phenomena, enhancing the capacity of self-observation, and richly facilitating the reconstructive process.

[[RunningHead]]

Transference-Countertransference

Hans Loewald M.D.

Hans Loewald

Hans W. Loewald, M.D.
65 Trumbull Street
New Haven, Connecticut 06510

Hans Loewald  

ABSTRACT

This paper concerns the dynamics of transference-countertransference as they reveal themselves in object relations and specifically in the psychoanalytic process. It is postulated that transference and countertransference cannot be viewed separately, that both analyst and patient exhibit transference-countertransference reactions, and that they are normal ingredients of the psychoanalytic process. Brief clinical illustrations are provided. Attention is called to special problems when the patient's defenses are primitive, and to the therapeutic value of the analyst's countertransference.

Institutional Problems of Psychoanalytic Education

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, New York 10605

Otto Kernberg  

ABSTRACT

I believe that psychoanalytic education is suffering from serious disturbances, which, by analogy, might be examined as an illness affecting the educational structures of psychoanalytic institutes and societies. After describing the symptoms of this illness, I shall explore its causes and suggest a possible course of treatment. My objective is not to present "ideal solutions" to problems, but to provide a theoretical frame that might facilitate such solutions.

[[RunningHead]]

Recontact with the Analyst Following Termination: A Survey of Seventy-One Cases

George Hartlaub M.D. , Gary Martin M.D. and Mark Rhine M.D.

Mark Rhine

George H. Hartlaub, M.D.
Gary C. Martin, M.D.
Mark W. Rhine, M.D.
469 South Cherry Street
Denver, Colorado 80222

Mark Rhine  

ABSTRACT

A questionnaire was sent to graduate members of the Denver Psychoanalytic Society asking for their experience with patients making contact with them after the analysis. They reported that within three years after termination two thirds of "successfully analyzed" patients had recontacted them. Seventy-one cases were surveyed. Most contacts were brief and did not seem to be the result of incomplete analysis. Rather, they aimed to satisfy ongoing needs for: (1) the continuing deidealization of the analyst, (2) the reactivation of the self-analytic function, and 93) the restructuring of self- and object representations by reporting developmentally significant accomplishments to the former analyst. The analyst's acknowledgement appears to be an integral part of the restructuring. This is work which either cannot be done before termination or the need for which has not been recognized during the analysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sense of Defect

Stanley Coen M.D.

Stanley Coen

Stanley J. Coen, M.D.
305 West End Avenue
New York, New York 10023

Stanley Coen  

ABSTRACT

The sense of defect, physical or psychological, is here regarded as a fantasy, a compromise formation, rather than an actual defect existing somewhere in the body, or in some psychological structure. It need not correlate with the reality to an external observer of physical or psychological disability. Psychoanalytic emphasis is directed to the motivations for the persistence of this sense of defect, rather than primarily to etiology (trauma) or genetic history. Accurate perception and evaluation, having been interdicted during childhood, is avoided with the magical hope that thereby one will be acceptable and what is wrong will disappear. The interaction between self and parents, real and fantasied, cannot be clearly known. The patient remains uncertain as to what is wrong, in whom it is located, and how it has come about. The sense of defect, physical or psychological, becomes the nidus around which much is crystallized. This is then offered up to a parent (and the superego) as an appeasement and seduction, aiming to help this parent feel better by agreeing that the child is indeed the cause of what is wrong. Dangerously destructive (and sexual) wishes are defended against and atoned for by emphasis on one's defectiveness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Three Visits to Eternity: Freud, Wiesel, and Patient X

Stanley Weiss M.D.

Stanley Weiss

Stanley S. Weiss, M.D.
1721 East 19th Avenue
Suite 528
Denver, Colorado 80218

Stanley Weiss  

ABSTRACT

Freud's experience on the Acropolis is reviewed and reappraised. Also, the experience of Elie Wiesel at the Wall in Jerusalem and Patient X's reaction visiting an Egyptian temple are examined. Carl Jung's wish to go to Rome and his inability to do so are noted. The aim of the paper is to offer deeper understanding about intense reactions many sensitive and creative people experience over travel to special places. These places are treated as idealized and ambivalently loved transference objects. Normal anticipatory pleasure prior to the trip is impaired and reality pleasure at the site cannot be enjoyed. When these spots are reached, ego regression is initiated by the intolerably intense narcissistic pleasure mobilized by the gratification of fantasies that were felt to be unrealizable. The fantasies can be conscious or unconscious and from oedipal as well as preoedipal and postoedipal developmental levels; however, they always involve the fulfillment of overwhelmingly powerful wishes. The deep ego regression, archaic fantasies, and the complex defenses mobilized are frightening since there may also be concern about ego dissolution or irreversible transformation. One highly adaptive solution which helps master these conflictual and developmental experiences is creative ego activity. While maintaining integrity for the individual ego and enhancing the self, creative work and accomplishment also enrich and advance the cultural process.

[[RunningHead]]

Determinism, Possibility, and Ethics

Edwin Wallace M.D.

Edwin Wallace

Edwin R. Wallace, IV, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry and Health Behavior Medical College of Georgia
1515 Pope Avenue
Augusta, Georgia 30912

Edwin Wallace  

ABSTRACT

This essay reexamines the age-old "determinism-free will" problem from a psychoanalytic perspective. The first section recapitulates the author's (1985) earlier argument on the nature of causation in psychoanalysis; the second part examines the compatibility of determinism and freedom; and the final section looks at the ethical ramifications of the issues at hand. The author exposits his adherence to universal determinism and attempts to answer the question, "What sort of possibility and ethics are permitted in a deterministic universe?"

[[RunningHead]]

Affect Availability, Tolerance, Complexity, and Modulation in Psychoanalysis: Followup of a Longitudinal, Prospective Study

Judy Kantrowitz Ph.D. , Frank Paolitto M.D. , Jerome Sashin M.D. , Leonard Solomon Ph.D. and Ann Katz

Ann Katz

Judy L. Kantrowitz, Ph.D.
Frank Paolitto, M.D.
Jerome Sashin, M.D.
Leonard Solomon, Ph.D.
Ann L. Katz, Ed.D.
334 Kent Street
Brookline, Massachusetts 02146

Ann Katz  

ABSTRACT

Evaluation of 22 patients in supervised psychoanalysis showed that affect availability and tolerance changed in a positive direction during the course of psychoanalysis. In this study, we have defined criteria of change that can be clinically observed. The changes observed were present one year following the completion of psychoanalysis. Ratings of analysts' and patients' interviews, and ratings of psychological tests all revealed that patients had derived "therapeutic benefit" in relation to affect experience and management. Discriminations were made among changes in affect modulation, which reflected both loosening and tightening of controls, changes in affect availability, changes in experience of painful affects, and changes in experience of affect complexity. Analysts' interviews and psychological test data reflected notable changes in affect modulation in the direction of increased control, while patients' interviews more often reflected changes in the direction of increased expressiveness.

ABSTRACT

Bulimia, the syndrome of food binging often followed by purging has been reported to be increasing in recent years. This communication reviews the psychoanalytic understanding of eating disorders, focusing on the pathology of early object relations and later intrapsychic conflicts over incestuous impregnation fantasies. Ancillary symptoms of kleptomania, laxative abuse, and vegetarianism are discussed, and associations with masturbatory conflicts and early adolescent phallic activity, i.e., horseback riding, are elaborated.

Borderline Personality Disorders: Research Issues and New Empirical Findings

Philip Holzman Ph.D. and J. Perry M.D.

J. Perry

Philip Holzman, Ph.D.
J. Christopher Perry, M.D.
The Cambridge Hospital
1493 Cambridge Street
Cambridge, Massachusetts 02139

J. Perry  

HOLZMAN INTRODUCED THE PANEL BY describing the history behind the decision to present a panel on empirical research at the scientific session. The 1981 joint task force on Careers in Psychoanalytic Research, chaired by Holzman, stated psychoanalysis "requires the nourishment which comes from those who devote themselves essentially full-time to the research effort. Without this group, psychoanalysis is in danger of falling behind in its scientific status." They recommended that the scientific program include a panel on research, to which the Program Committee, chaired by Richard A. Isay, was receptive. This inaugural panel focuses on the study of the borderline syndrome, which is of central interest to psychoanalysts. The purpose is to determine how to integrate clinical research and new findings with psychoanalytic concepts, or to determine how they might alter these concepts.

Holzman noted that diagnosis is a way to systematize knowledge. DSM-III borderline personality disorder is a diagnosis that includes a heterogeneous group of clinical phenomena. Systematic research on this is rare in comparison to other disorders, while speculation and dogmatic assertion abound but have not often been put to reliable test.

Gunderson presented his paper, "Interfaces between psychoanalytic and empirical studies of borderline personality." While the original descriptions of the borderline patients emphasized intrapsychic characteristics, borderline personality disorder was included in DSM-III because of a growing series of empirical studies that clarified its boundaries.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December 16, 1983.

Panelists: Arnold M. Cooper, John E. Gunderson, Robert Michels, J. Christopher Perry.

Hartvig Dahl  

ABSTRACT

The author's purpose is threefold: first, to demonstrate the redundancy in the events of the complex life of a patient as told to a psychoanalyst; second, to show two novel ways to represent those events simply; and third, to suggest that the field of psychoanalysis, under assault from philosophers of science and hermeneuticists alike for either not being or for trying to be a science, can in principle be reclaimed for science. It can be done by abandoning a widely held belief that human behavior is simply too complex, too malleable, too rooted in ineffable experience to permit of systematic scientific capture.

Jung's Lost Contribution to the Dilemma of Narcissism

Jeffrey Satinover M.D.

Jeffrey Satinover

Jeffrey Satinover, M.D.
Child Study Center Yale University School of Medicine
Box 3333
New Haven, Connecticut 06510

Jeffrey Satinover  

ABSTRACT

This paper proposes a partial answer to the hypothetical question, "What might Jung's contribution to a theory of narcissism have been had he remained a psychoanalytic writer?" An attempt is made to demonstrate that, long before it became the vogue, Jung began to explore narcissistic terrain. In the course of this exploration, he formulated many insights, in idiosyncratic language, some of which have since been reformulated in psychoanalytic terms. It is posited that, in addition to anticipating current developments in our understanding of narcissism, Jung formulated some ideas that go beyond it. Whether or not they prove accurate, these ideas are an untapped psychoanalytic resource.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud: Objects and Structure

Allan Compton M.D.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton, M.D.
1081 Moraga Drive
Los Angeles, California 90049

Allan Compton  

ABSTRACT

This paper, part of a series exploring the development of Freud's object concept, deals with the concepts of objects and psychic structure in the period from 1915 to 1938. In connection with the increased prominence of object loss and hostility in his clinical material, the role of objects in the building of psychic structure via identification assumed increased importance from 1917 on. After introduction of the ego-id-superego model, Freud asserted for the first time that object choice does take place as early as the oedipal period, and interpolated the phallic-oedipal phase into his sequence of developmental phases. The prominence of structure formation through identification continued, but no new object concepts are discerned.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to William I. Grossman

Darius Ornston M.D.

Darius Ornston

Darius Ornston  

September, 1985

The traditional translation of Lustprinzip into "Pleasure Principle" does have a lot behind it. Freud's own ever-varying accounts of this ancient notion range—quite characteristically—between two poles: (1) Perceived sensations—people always try to avoid unpleasant sensations or to do what they expect to feel good. (2) Regulatory principles: all human activity seeks some constant rhythm, stability, Nirvana, or some such universal aim. The first of these is practically the same as Freud's assumption of psychic determinism, that is, all human behavior is motivated (Smith, 1977). The second was an attempt to divine some psycho-biological law analogous to the Laws of Thermodynamics. Often, Freud (1915) has it both ways at once: "The activities of the most highly developed psychic apparatus submit to the Lustprinzip, that is, are automatically regulated by sensations ranging from Lust to Unlust" (Empfindungen der Lust-Unlustreihe, G. W., 10, p. 214).

[[RunningHead]]

A Preliminary Report on Defenses and Conflicts Associated with Borderline Personality Disorder

J. Perry and Steven Cooper Ph.D.

Steven Cooper

J. Christopher Perry, M.P.H.
Steven H. Cooper, Ph.D.
1493 Cambridge Street
Cambridge, Massachusetts 02/39,

Steven Cooper  

ABSTRACT

The authors present preliminary psychodynamic findings from a naturalistic study of borderline personality disorder compared to antisocial personality disorder and bipolar type II (depression with hypomania) affective disorder. An independent psychodynamic interview of each subject was videotaped from which ratings were made of the presence of 22 defense mechanisms and 11 psychodynamic conflicts.

A factor analysis of ratings from 81 subjects supported the separation of borderline (splitting, projective identification) from narcissistic defenses (devaluation, omnipotence, idealization, mood-incongruent denial). While certain groups of defenses were associated with each diagnosis, defense ratings did not significantly discriminate the three diagnostic groups, suggesting a limit to their diagnostic value.

Among 27 subjects rated, borderline personality was strongly associated with two conflicts: separation-abandonment, and a global conflict over the experience and expression of emotional needs and anger. Antisocial personality was psychodynamically distinct and more heterogeneous. Bipolar type II was associated with two hypothesized depressive conflicts: dominant other and dominant goal. Chronic depression, which was more common in both personality disorder groups than in bipolar type II, was associated with a third depressive conflict, overall gratification inhibition. Overall, conflicts were powerful discriminators of the three diagnostic groups. The heuristic value of these findings is discussed.

The Psychoanalysis of the Older Patient

Stanley Cath M.D. and Nancy Miller Ph.D.

Nancy Miller

Stanley Cath, M.D.
Nancy E. Miller, Ph.D.
National Institute of Mental Health (Room 11-C-03)
5600 Fishers Lane
Rockville, Maryland 20857

Nancy Miller  

IN HIS OPENING REMARKS, DR. STANLEY CATH, who chaired the panel in his absence, gratefully acknowledged the contributions of George Pollock in initiating and organizing this panel. Cath underscored that in the psychoanalytic literature, there has been a long delayed appreciation of the developmental potential inherent in the middle and later years. He noted that this oversight arose in part as a result of Freud's initial admonition against taking patients past fifty into analytic treatment. Although Freud indeed commented on the "inelasticity" of elderly people and stressed the difficulties in working through a lifetime of cumulative trauma and experience, it is nevertheless useful to recall that he formulated these sentiments during a period of profound grief over the loss of a beloved child, and at a time clouded by the onset of the malignant illness that was later to claim his life. Despite therapeutic pessimism regarding the prospects for change in middle and late life, and despite chronic premonitions regarding intimations of his own demise, Freud's conceptualizations regarding the criteria for analyzability remain as cogent and valid today as when they were written. These criteria, Cath stated, will serve as the best guides to prognosis, rather than a focus on chronological age. Evidence is accumulating from all over the world suggesting that chronological age, contrary to Freud's impression, is not a valid predictor of analyzability; that throughout evanescence and senescence, new assimilation, leading to modifications of psychic structure, and new enthusiasm and vitality, forged in the psychoanalytic crucible, remain possible. Aging is not a monolithic experience for all persons, and in late life, as in youth, it is just as important that potential cases for analysis be thoughtfully and appropriately selected.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 19, 1982.

Panelists: Earl J. Simburg, Pearl King, Anne-Marie Sandler, Nancy E. Miller; Discussant: Martin A. Berezin.

Freud, Dora, and the Maid: A Study of Countertransference

Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jules Glenn, M.D.
8 Preston Road
Great Neck, New York 11023

Jules Glenn  

ABSTRACT

Early advances in psychoanalytic knowledge, profound though they were, were incomplete structures to be built upon, modified, and partially discarded. In addition to errors due to insufficient knowledge, Freud's difficulties with Dora stemmed from countertransference. Dora's transference included an identification with a governess/maid. Important oedipal role played by a nursemaid in Freud's life made him vulnerable to being left by Dora. The maid, Monika, "the prime originator" of Freud's neurosis, seduced him, chastised him, and taught him of hell. In his self-analysis she was associated with Freud's mother who left him when she gave birth to his sister. When he was two and a half years old, Monika was discharged and jailed for stealing.

I suggest that Freud's attraction to Dora revealed itself in his libidinal imagery of the treatment and his premature sexual interpretations, the effects of which he misjudged. Defending against his attraction, he pushed her away from him, did not act to keep her in analysis or allow her to reenter analysis later. In addition, since Dora had left him as he must have felt his childhood nursemaid had, he reacted as if she were that maid. Hurt, saddened, and angered, he used reversal and deserted her, thus damping his feelings.

Theodore Shapiro M.D. and Martin Weich M.D.

Theodore Shapiro, M.D.

Martin Weich  

IN HIS INTRODUCTION, THEODORE SHAPIRO cited Freud's long interest in language and symbol formation and his intense curiosity about how the role of language and thought supersede the need to act in the psychoanalytic situation. During the late 1950's, the linguist Noam Chomsky created a revolution in academia which spread to psychology and even to psychoanalysis. In the wake of that revolution, Victor Rosen constructed a panel on symbol formation for the American Psychoanalytic Association and formed a linguistics study group in New York, thus establishing forums of discussion between linguists and psychoanalysts. Chomsky himself was one of those who entered into the interdisciplinary dialogue at the American Psychoanalytic meeting. The changing focus from semantics and meaning to syntactics and formal structures that Chomsky espoused has since evolved to a current interest in what linguists call pragmatics, namely issues of use and intention in actual linguistic expression. This is a radical shift from formal analysis of grammar and the search for a minimal set of rules that will account for any possible sentence. There has been a parallel change between what has gone on within linguistics and what has occurred within psychoanalysis. While the last language panel at the American Psychoanalytic meeting eight years ago, run by Shapiro, discussed the rule-directed behavior involved in both linguistics and psychoanalysis, the relation of language to thought processes, and also linguistic relativity. The focus of the present panel, he stated, would be on aspects of how analysts make interpretations and how these interventions and the forms they take influence what goes on in the treatment process. "We would like to know what elements actually affect the patient—how what is said becomes efficacious

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 18, 1983.

Panelists: John E. Gedo, Warren S. Poland, Martin J. Weich; Discussant: Hartvig Dahl.

On the Nature of the Oceanic Experience

David Werman M.D.

David Werman

David S. Werman, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry Duke University Medical Center
Box 3812
Durham, North Carolina 27710

David Werman  

ABSTRACT

It is postulated that consciousness is not simply an organ of perception, but that it possesses a structure, or organization, despite its enormous fluidity. In support of these views, the oceanic experience is explored. It demonstrates the impact, on this state of consciousness, of the subject's values and culture. Like the oceanic experience, every state of consciousness—what is in awareness at any given time—is a complex phenomenon that derives from all aspects of the psyche, including the subject's value system and the influences of his culture.

Leo Stone M.D.

Leo Stone

Leo Stone, M.D.
One Gracie Terrace
New York, New York 10028

Leo Stone  

ABSTRACT

This paper has two general sources: (1) my own clinical psychoanalytic work extending over about four and one half decades; and (2) consideration of certain outstandingly influential ideas in our literature: first, those of the pioneer contributors, and then those of certain more recent writers, whose views have sometimes exhibited important differences from those widely held in the past. I try to evaluate the two groups—in their occasional overlapping and important divergences, in relation to my own clinical experience. A certain general position of my own emerges from such process inevitably; but it is far from "revolutionary." Indeed, the contrary is more largely true. In extreme anticipatory condensation—what I do propose, from my own reflections, is the preeminent importance of an archaic characterological core in depressive illness.

The First Relationship

By Daniel Stern. Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard University Press, 1977, 160 pp., $8.95 (paperback, $3.95).

Sanford Gifford M.D.

Sanford Gifford

Sanford Gifford, M.D.
Cambridge, Massachusetts

Sanford Gifford  

For many analysts who are not engaged in early developmental research, this remarkable little book will come as a delightful surprise. The First Relationship has made its way almost unnoticed onto the reserve shelves of our institute libraries, university bookstores, and our candidates' backpacks. Stern's widest audience, in fact, probably lies among both students and teachers of psychoanalysis and analysts of all ages who are interested in analytic theory and the current state of infant observation. His fellow researchers in the field will already be familiar with his many papers over the past decade, and the book is not intended, as he himself states, to be a popular guide to child upbringing, another "how to" book. Neither is it a highly technical research monograph, full of tables and reaction times, suitable only for specialists, although Stern is a leader in the use of advanced technical methods of observation, based on filmed observations, freeze-frame analyses of expressive movements, and measurements in milliseconds.

What Stern has accomplished with remarkable skill is a weaving together of his findings on early mother-infant interactions into a lively narrative account of the first year of life, from the first expressive repertories of mutual response, still describable in terms derived from animal behavior, to the "interpersonal experience of being with the other person" that can be called a human relationship. This process from an undifferentiated but "preadapted" phase of ego development to a phase of object constancy, from interacting infant-mother behaviors to the infant's acquisition of an inner representation of the mother distinct from all others, occupies the period from birth to the ninth or tenth month. But Stern's freshest and most surprising observations come from the third to the sixth month, and his focus, unlike that of others who have studied feeding, sleep rhythms, or other aspects of infant care, is centered on those intervals of playful interchange, "having fun together," that organize sensory experience, communicate emotion, and create the first object relationship. These periods of spontaneous exchange between mother and infant, occurring at any time during other caretaking procedures or intentionally elicited by the mother, are brief and infrequent at first, and gradually evolve into those characteristic social dialogues that provide both the pleasurable excitement and the organization of sensory experience that are necessary for the infant's healthy development.

ABSTRACT

Concepts of self of Grossman, Kohut, and Kernberg are briefly summarized and some limitations suggested. The author proposes a theoretical basis of the concepts of self and authentic self parallel to superego development and the concept of ownership of impulses and values.

The Child Dream and the Child Transference

Don De Francisco M.D.

Don De Francisco

Don De Francisco, M.D.
19712 MacArthur Boulevard (Suite 220)
Irvine, California 92715

Don De Francisco  

ABSTRACT

This paper explores the symbolic meaning of dreams in which children appear with special attention to the way children in dreams symbolize the self, particularly the dependent and developing self. It is suggested that patients' growth in analysis can be monitored by observing what happens to the children in their dreams. This paper also explores the vicissitudes of the child transference, in which the patient treats the analyst as a child. An analysis is described in which the child dream and the child transference played an important role in elucidating the patient's neurotic behaviors. The author contends that the child dream and the child transference are common and clinically useful phenomena, especially important in the analysis of dependency conflicts. An additional thesis of this paper is that the child transference is most likely to be found in instances where a patient played a parental role with one of their parents during childhood.

The Psychoanalytic Study of Society, Vol. 9

Edited by Werner Muensterberger, L. Bryce Boyer, and Simon A. Grolnick. New York: Psychohistory Press, 1982, 348 pp., $28.50.

Mark Gehrie Ph.D.

Mark Gehrie

Mark J. Gehrie, Ph.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Mark Gehrie  

This volume is representative of a spectrum of approaches that characterize the effort to apply psychoanalysis outside of its fundamental clinical context. Each of the contributions reflects its authors' unique

733

view of the relation between psychoanalytic theory, method, and the interpretation of data. There is vast diversity here, to the extent that there appears to be some justice in thinking of "Applied Psychoanalysis" as a collection of informed but idiosyncratic efforts, rather than as a coherent discipline.

This collection is divided into sections titled Anthropology, Religion, Mythology, Literature, Political Science, and finally the report of a Panel Discussion on Psychoanalysis, Folklore, and Socialization Processes. Each section is devoted to an area of content or a discipline to which psychoanalysis in some manner has been applied as an interpretive source. The contributors also come from a range of disciplines, including psychoanalysis. Each has chosen a subject from within his own field, to which psychoanalytic theory is then applied. Since the nature of how this is accomplished varies so widely from chapter to chapter, and limitations of space do not permit a full review of all sixteen papers, I have selected a few which permit some discussion of the relevant issues.

Perhaps the most outstanding issue in the kind of work represented here is that of distance from the kind of inference that is characteristic of psychoanalytic clinical practice. Unlike clinical work, in which criteria for understanding and interpretation are (comparatively speaking) relatively clear-cut, in applied analysis there is a continuum in the degree or extent of leaps of inference that different authors permit. As a result, there is a corresponding range in the degree of conviction that different interpretations about these non-clinical data will offer.

Other issues that stand out in the consideration of such material are those which are also familiar in the clinical context. They include relations between what is conscious and what is unconscious, what is manifest and what is latent, and what is real and what is fantasy. If, for example, we decide (as a number of contributors to this volume do) to treat myths as if they were dreams belonging to an individual, where does this analogy carry us? With a patient, we listen carefully to the dream and the associations, and place this psychic event in the context of the transference and the analytic and developmental histories before offering an interpretation. We then listen with equal care to the response to the interpretation, as another measure of its correctness. With a piece of cultural content such as a myth or a ritual, it is tempting to ignore what in the clinical context we consider to be indispensable.
